data.title: U.S. Dollar Stakes Get Raised - What To Do Now In The Rates Space?
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas

The US dollar popped weaker on most crosses late Tuesday. This stemmed from off-the-cuff comments from President Trump along the lines that he's okay with some dollar softness, as it means that other currencies are less likely to devalue versus the dollar, in turn preventing their products from becoming more competitively priced in the US.

This harps back to the so-called Mar-a-Largo accord that voiced frustration that dollar reserve status, while positive, resulted in a dollar that was too strong, in turn making US manufacturing less competitive. Our views on that are here (Q&A).

Remember, it's the US Treasury that has responsibility for the US dollar, not the Federal Reserve. Scott Bessent, on CNBC on Wednesday morning, cited the "strong dollar policy" phrase. So, he has decided not to push in the same direction as President Trump.

Indeed, the dollar does not need a push. It's already moving along the path of least resistance - weaker. It's the unwritten preference of the administration, but not too weak too fast, and not getting to uncomfortable levels. Our FX strategists have also maintained a weaker dollar view.

So what does all this mean for the rates versus FX arbitrage? In recent weeks, we've opined on this for Japan and South Korea and China. Now we focus on the eurozone, or more specifically, Ester verus SOFR.

The FX exposure piece - anticipating a weaker dollar

A weakening in the US dollar versus the euro should not be a huge surprise. It's discounted in the FX forward profile, which is a reflection of the rate differentials between higher US rates and lower eurozone rates. If those spreads represent an equilibrium, then the EUR must appreciate versus the USD. That said, forwards are not always realised.

A great example of that is the recent weakness of the Japanese yen. There, the forwards have not been realised as the rate spreads have not represented an equilibrium. Hence, the rapid tightening in spreads, mostly from higher Japanese market rates.

The big question from an Ester versus SOFR perspective is how the EUR/USD FX rate actually evolves versus the forwards. Our FX strategists have generally viewed the 1.20 to 1.25 range as a path we can absolutely venture along. Beyond that, it gets more hazy.

Anything that threatened 1.30 would look a tad outlandish. So conservatively, we have 1.25 as a critical level, acting as something of a floor. As we glean along the FX forward profile (chart below), we hit 1.24 on the 3yr tenor and we're at 1.26 on a 4yr tenor. That's the area of inflection from a rates tenor perspective.

The EUR/USD exchange rate - shooting to 1.20 area (USD down!)

And for FX forward profile - discounting 1.36, eventually

The spreads piece - steady to tighter anticipated

The bias from the previous section is to avoid being short the euro versus the dollar on tenors out to 3.5yrs, as there is a material risk that the actual EUR/USD FX rate dips below the FX forward profile. If that happens, and we're long carry (receive SOFR and pay Ester), the value of that carry is wiped out (as we have created a EUR liability that gets hurt on EUR strength). In that case, it's best to be short carry (receive Ester and pay SOFR). That carry cost ranges from -c.160bp in the 1yr out to -c.140bp in the 3yr (see charts below).

For longer tenors, the implied breakeven for long carry plays is above 1.25 (as gleaned from the FX forward profile above). The area of real comfort is in the 7yr tenor, which has an FX forward rate of 1.31 (and the 10yr is even more comfortable at 1.36).

The simple prognosis here is long carry is comfortable in longer tenors, provided there is a reasonable degree of comfort that EUR/USD does not venture above 1.25, with the 1.3 area as the ultimate pain point.

US spreads over eurozone (Ester into SOFR)

Chart on right includes the cross currency basis - practically invisible (as at or around zero)

The basis piece is not a factor, but the FX entry point actually is

The EUR/USD cross-currency basis, which is effectively an arbitrage between actual spreads and FX forwards, is practically zero at the moment. It used to reflect a sizeable dollar premium, which resulted in wider spreads than advertised (often, typically by some 30bp).

That has disappeared and is liable to remain absent. That's good for the negative carry play, as it means no amplification of spreads. While for positive carry plays, it means that a prior positive arbitrage has gone, but at least has not flipped to a space where it actually hurts.

One clear plus for the positive carry play is the FX entry point has improved. Get in at as strong an EUR/USD rate as possible, and the risk for subsequent EUR/USD strength is reduced. Also, the FX forward profile shifts higher too, marking out a better breakeven prognosis.

With respect to absolute spreads, the table below shows that absolute spreads are a tad lower versus their historical averages (by some 10bp). Still good entry points on absolute spreads ranging from 125bp to 150bp. And we expect these spreads to be biased tighter, towards the 100-125bp area, mostly from the Ester side. But not much in it.

Here are the detailed numbers...

Performance Overview

In the quarter, Health Care, Communication Services, and Information Technology contributed the most to absolute returns. Alphabet (GOOGL), which was among the portfolio’s largest positions for much of 2025, continued its strong run in the fourth quarter with accelerating fundamentals augmented by further re-rating in the stock. Danaher (DHR) rallied to close the year as business fundamentals regained their long-term trajectory after a prolonged downturn in the industry. Lam Research (LRCX), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), and Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) were also top-five contributors to returns. The largest detractors from absolute quarterly returns were Financials, Industrials, and Real Estate. Fiserv (FISV) declined significantly in October, contributing meaningfully to the fund’s underperformance. Cadence (CDNS), Linde (LIN), United Rentals (URI), and Progressive (PGR) rounded out the top-five detractors in the quarter.

For the full year of 2025, the top-five contributors to returns were Lam Research, Alphabet, Amphenol (APH), Taiwan Semiconductor, and ASML (ASML), and the top-five detractors from returns were Fiserv, Chipotle (CMG), Constellation Software (CNSWF), Roper (ROP), and Floor & Decor (FND). Our strategy’s relative underperformance in the year was more than explained by Fiserv and our underweight in Western Europe.

In a surprise move, Fiserv’s new management reset their operating model and communicated systematic over-earning under prior management, who, it appears, prioritized near-term revenue streams and cost-cutting initiatives rather than long-term, durable growth. In NZS terms, the previous team had been sacrificing duration and longevity for near-term efficiency, which is the opposite of what we look for in investments. Our lesson with Fiserv is one we’ve unfortunately been taught before: a good company trading at a great valuation does not equal a resilient position. While we remain valuation disciplined, the prediction of future earnings can be quite fragile to ranges of outcomes around the core business (to the downside and the upside). An attractive valuation cannot compensate for unhealth in the organization, just as great companies can often make expensive valuations appear cheap in retrospect. While we appreciate the new CEO of Fiserv “coming clean”, with investors, we exited the position as it became clear that we had evaluated the company incorrectly and that it was neither resilient nor optional.

In Western Europe, banks, pharmaceuticals, energy, defense, and tobacco companies had a strong year of performance. We generally do not find investments that fit our framework in those specific industries. The region also experienced a material tailwind to USD returns from a strengthening euro. For the time being, we are comfortable with our positioning in every geography, and we will continue to scour the globe for adaptable businesses that are creating non-zero-sum outcomes in their ecosystems.

Portfolio Positioning

After adding to IT names amid weakness in the first half of 2025, we began reallocating outside of IT in the third quarter as AI euphoria returned, multiples in the sector recovered, and we found incrementally more attractive opportunities in other sectors like software (see below). In the backdrop of a myopically focused market, we are finding a growing opportunity set of left-behind, “quality growth” companies trading at attractive valuations. In the quarter, we added several names to the portfolio that would loosely fit this description – Danaher and Linde to name two – and introduced new positions with Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG), an insurance broker, and Dino Polska (DNOPF), a fast-growing Polish grocer. New optionality positions were also started with JFrog (FROG) and Heartflow (HTFL). In the quarter, we exited Fiserv, Chipotle, and monday.com. The portfolio remains overweight IT, although at a level below where we entered 2025.

Artificially Disrupted?

As recently as 12 months ago, valuations for cloud software companies were among the highest in the market. This reflected the long-time fundamentals of the sector: attractive growth rates, sticky and predictable revenue streams, enviable gross margins with little marginal costs, negative working capital, etc. In 2025, the market created a wall of worry that AI would displace existing software companies based on the assumption that white-collar job losses and disruption from AI agents would obsolete some of the most important companies in the world.

We agree the range of outcomes has widened, but we think there is reason to be more optimistic about the software industry’s ability to adapt to the new age of AI. The first indicator of adaptability we look for is non-zero sumness, or NZS. We think software systems of record (SoR) are generally starting from a position of positive NZS – they are often mission critical, deeply embedded in an organization, and contain the key data and workflow context needed to transform a workflow through AI; however, they generally represent a low-single-digit expense as a percentage of revenue. Vertical market software platforms further leverage domain expertise to deliver purpose-built functionality for their respective niches and to inform which features to prioritize. In essence, customers of B2B software rely heavily on their SoR and generally are not itching to ditch them.

In a customer’s ideal world, they would leverage AI within their existing software. Here, another foundational element of adaptability for cloud software is its ability to be frequently versioned. Potentially driven by paranoia and/or reacting to stock price declines, we have seen software platforms relatively aggressively introduce AI functionality. This augmentation has occurred either through internal development on top of the foundational models or via APIs for third-party tools. Customer adoption of these features has been mixed, but we expect continued progress in 2026 and beyond. The important point is that the existing SoR do not appear to be complacent and are ensuring that their users can productively leverage AI within their existing workflows. So long as this is the case, we think startup competitors will face the same historical challenges in their attempt to displace the incumbents. Intense competition is not new in most categories of software, after all.

In summary, do we think AI has widened the range of outcomes for software? Yes. Do we think the market has taken a rather draconian position on the subject? Probably. We also believe there is a compelling fundamental bull case in the range of outcomes where AI leads to reduced churn, new revenue streams, and accelerated operating margin expansion (software companies are arguably the biggest beneficiaries of AI efficiency gains). In the short term, we expect software’s day-to-day moves to be driven by the markets’ fears of AI and decelerating fundamentals; however, longer-term, we think software represents one of the more interesting opportunities in the market. Our portfolio’s software positioning remains focused primarily on SoR and vertical market software, which we think have the best chance of adapting.

The following market commentary is excerpted from NZS Capital Investor, Brad Slingerlend’s, December Newsletter, SITALWeek #460

We are no strangers to bubbles and narrow markets, having invested through some interesting times during the last several decades. Indeed, our Complexity Investing framework was created to explain how to invest in an unpredictable world. One strategy we use in times like this is to shift narrow predictions to broader predictions, both at the individual stock level and the overall portfolio level. At one point, a few months ago, I expressed to our investors that the market was evolving into a single prediction: one AI startup was going to build a multi-hundred-billion-dollar business in just 2-3 years. Could it happen? Sure, but is it the most likely outcome? Probably not. Are we concerned about circular revenue (aka vendor financing, as we called it during the telecom bubble) and increased leverage? Yes. The fact that everyone seems to be a consenting participants doesn’t make it OK. It reminds me of the great Penn & Teller cup-and-ball trick done with transparent cups. Even in plain sight, it’s still a trick. There is no better metaphor for market bubbles than the transparent cup and balls – despite being in on the trick, it still fools you. Our job as investors today, in terms of how we think about the world at NZS Capital, is to identify and seek to take advantage of the broader (higher probability) predictions that may be connected to the markets’ AI gamble. For example, we can create a more agnostic portfolio in which we carefully match positions to the range of outcomes encompassed by two diametrically opposed world views: in one world, AI meets investors’ high expectations and we are off to the races; in the other, classic negative feedback loops and efficiency gains create a more predictable, linear progress curve for AI’s impact on the world, economy, and markets. My hope is actually for a linear impact, because I think it’s important that the pace of change is slow enough to allow entrepreneurial job creation to replace those lost to AI. But, as they say, hope in one hand and spit in the other, and see which fills up faster.

That tongue-in-cheek aside, I am not, in fact, overly negative about the bumpy road ahead. Indeed, I’ve found it much more productive to view the world through the lens of skeptical optimism. As I’ve noted in the past, neither cynicism nor pessimism have been winning long-term investment strategies. Skepticism is always prudent, but optimism is the only way to make money in the long term. In many ways, it’s nearly impossible to have a successful investment strategy or philosophy that expresses pessimism when the world is optimistic. It does work occasionally, just as a stopped clock is right twice a day. However, by being optimistic when the world is pessimistic, you are more likely to collect the treasure that’s lying around the economic landscape. You don’t even need to find the end of the rainbow. So, now is a good time to prepare to be optimistic for when the world turns pessimistic. It was Buffett who famously said: “be fearful when others are greedy, and be greedy when others are fearful”. I think the first part of Buffett’s aphorism is a tough way to make a living as an investor, given that things are always getting better over time, and the second half sounds a little too cynical to me. So, with a great deal of unmerited hubris, let’s rewrite that quote as: “Be appropriately skeptical when others are optimistic, be optimistic when others are pessimistic, and never be cynical”. OK, I admit, Buffett’s is a tad more catchy. Another bit of traditional advice for long-term investors, often trumpeted by Buffett in the past, is to own a passive index like the S&P 500. I wonder if that suggestion needs some updating as well, given the current, narrow prediction that’s overtaken the stock markets? At the moment, the top five stocks in the S&P 500 are around 30% of the index, and they are all highly correlated with each other (NVIDIA, Apple, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Amazon). Is that a diversified long-term portfolio, or is it a risky, concentrated bet on one theme? A more appropriate approach today might be closer to the agnostic – but still very active – portfolio construction mentioned above. The good news for investors today is that the narrow, single-prediction stock market has resulted in some fantastic, high quality growth businesses being left for dead across many sectors (for example, software, where I believe systems of record and vertical market software are well positioned for either AI path the world may take).

This discussion of ephemeral bubbles reminds me of one of my favorite quotes from one of my favorite movies, Harvey (based on the original play). At one point Elwood P. Dowd, played by Jimmy Stewart, is at a bar, perhaps with a 6’2” tall seemingly invisible rabbit, and Mr. Wilson from the sanitarium asks the bartender about Dowd, “Is he alone?” To which the bartender replies, “Well, there’s two schools of thought, sir”. Right now, there are very much two schools of thought. Perhaps that mischievous pooka is real, or perhaps not, but we certainly all want to believe in the big bunny. So, as the evening wears on, we remain skeptically optimistic for the near future as we see how the AI platform wars shake out. Over the longer time horizon, we are giddy for the explosion of new ideas, companies, and scientific revolutions that the massive AI platforms will enable, which I look forward to writing about in the coming years.

Conclusion

We’re deeply grateful for your continued support and trust , which is central to everything we do. The year 2025 marked NZS’s sixth year of operations, and we are greatly encouraged by the engagement we’ve had from existing, new, and prospective investors. We feel extremely fortunate to have grown the business, exiting 2025 with >$4B under management for the first time. We’re energized by the opportunity set for the portfolio and NZS Capital as we enter 2026.

As always, we welcome your questions, comments, thoughts, and ideas.

The NZS Team

data.title: Lyrical Asset Management 2025 U.S. Value Review Letter
data.description:
2025 was a good year for Lyrical with our CS composite generating a 17.9% net return, matching the S&P 500 and significantly outperforming the S&P 500 Value by 470 bps. Keeping pace with the S&P 500 was difficult this year. The index is capitalization-weighted, and its returns were inflated by outsized contributions from a small number of very large growth stocks. Those mega cap stocks skewed the index return so much that 70% of S&P 500 constituents underperformed, and the S&P 500 Equal Weight index returned only 11.4%, 6.5 percentage points less than the S&P 500 and Lyrical.

In large part due to the AI trade, the cap-weighted S&P 500 has now outperformed the equal weight version by a record amount over the last three years. And while it has been most extreme over that period, the cycle of cap weighted outperformance has had a longer history dating back to March 2015. Cumulatively, the outperformance has become substantial. Over nearly 11 years, the S&P 500 has returned 300%, 109 percentage points ahead of the 191% return of the S&P 500 Equal Weight.

Importantly, this excess return of the S&P 500 was not driven by faster earnings growth, but rather due to a soaring valuation multiple. This leaves the S&P 500 index very expensive by historical standards, and at a lofty 32% premium relative to the equal weight index at year end.

Unless the future growth of the cap-weighted index significantly outpaces the equal weight index, we would expect the S&P 500 valuation to fall back in line with the equal weight index and its own history, unwinding the thousands of basis points of outperformance it has generated over the last few years. It is easy to forget that the equal weight index has been the better performer historically, and that to date, every time mega caps have outperformed, they eventually went on to underperform, losing all their outperformance and then some. If that happens, we believe it would be a very favorable environment for our value investing approach.

Even without S&P 500 valuation reversion, we are confident in our ability to perform given the growth and valuation characteristics of our portfolio. Our current portfolio has a history of 10.6% EPS growth, four percentage points faster than the 6.6% growth of the S&P 500. Yet, despite that faster growth, our portfolio also has an attractive valuation of only 12.5x forward earnings at year end, while the slower growing S&P 500 valuation is 78% higher at 22.2x.

If our companies continue to grow as fast or faster than the S&P 500, the wide valuation discount should justifiably narrow, delivering outperformance. That is the Lyrical formula for success.

S&P 500 Performance: Capitalization vs. Equal Weight

Historically, the equal weight S&P 500 has outperformed the cap-weighted S&P 500 over time. As we show below in the graph on the left, from the inception of the S&P 500 Equal Weight in 1990 through March 2015, a period of about 25 years, the equal weight index outperformed the S&P 500 by 1.9 percent per annum.

However, as shown in the graph on the right, over the last ~11 years since then, it is the cap weighted index that has outperformed by an average of 3.3 percent per annum, and this has lowered the historical outperformance of the S&P 500 Equal Weight to just 0.3 percent per annum over the full 36-year history.

Source: FactSet

Within the last 11 years of cap weight outperformance, there are clearly two distinct periods, which are also shown above in the graph on the right. For the first 8 years, the cap-weighted index mildly outperformed by 80 basis points per year. But over the last three years the cap-weighted index outperformance has been massive at more than 1,000 basis points per year. In fact, this three-year period is even more extreme than the tech bubble era and the most extreme we have seen in the entire history of the equal weight index.

Is it sustainable?

In total, the 3.3% annualized outperformance of the cap weight index over these last 11 years compounds to 109 percentage points! The question going forward is whether cap weighted outperformance is sustainable. As we analyze the drivers of outperformance over the last 11 years, our conclusion is that it is not.

Earnings for both indices have been almost identical. In the left-hand graph below, we show the growth in forward earnings of the S&P 500 and S&P 500 Equal Weight over the 11-year period that the cap weight index has been outperforming. Both indices grew at essentially the same rate, such that over these 11 years, the cap weight earnings are only 1.6% higher than the Equal Weight earnings, an annualized difference of about 10 basis points per year. Thus, fundamental earnings growth did not explain the huge performance difference.

Nearly all the outperformance was driven by the valuation of the S&P 500 becoming historically expensive. In the right-hand graph below, we show the change in valuation for the two indices. Eleven years ago, the two indices had similar multiples. In fact, the equal weight index multiple was one point higher at 17.3x compared to 16.2x for the cap-weighted index. At the end of 2025, the multiple comparison was quite different. Despite essentially the same historical growth over the past 11 years, the cap-weighted index multiple is now 32% higher. That is a massive premium to pay for something that has not historically offered materially better growth.

Source: FactSet

Compared to the equal weight index, and its own history, the S&P 500 multiple simply looks too high. Unless the future growth of the cap-weighted index significantly outpaces the equal weight index, we would expect the valuation multiple to fall back in line with the equal weight index and its own history, unwinding the thousands of basis points of outperformance it has generated over the last few years.

How did we keep up?

At Lyrical, we have not owned any of the mega cap growth stocks that have disproportionately driven the S&P 500 returns, yet we still were able to match the S&P 500 performance and significantly outperform the S&P 500 Equal Weight and S&P 500 Value indices.

While we have not owned any of the expensive AI-related mega-cap growth stocks, our top four contributors in 2025 were attractively valued companies that benefitted from the expected spending on AI technology. These stocks, shown in the table below, included NRG Energy (an independent power producer), Johnson Controls (a provider of HVAC products and services), FLEX (a contract manufacturer and leading provider of critical equipment to hyperscalers), and United Rentals (the world’s largest construction equipment rental company).

Source: FactSet

These stocks entered 2025 at an average discount to the S&P 500 of over 30% and some are still undervalued heading into 2026. Despite its 79% net return, NRG Energy (NRG) is still attractively valued at just 13.7x forward earnings when we normalize depreciation expense and add the accretion from the company’s LS Power acquisition. Flex (FLEX) is also still attractive after a net return of 57%. Flex finished the year with a forward P/E of 17.5x, a 21% discount to the S&P 500 despite its much higher growth.

Closer to fair value is Johnson Controls (JCI), which ended the year with a forward P/E of 22.8x adjusting for non-cash amortization. Lastly, United Rentals (URI) reached our estimate of fair value in September after a 36% increase year-to-date, and we sold it from the portfolio.

Not all earnings growth was rewarded

While our AI-related value stocks provided a boost to 2025 returns, severe multiple compression in a large portion of the portfolio kept us from having an even better year.

Throughout the year, fundamentals were strong across our portfolio. Apart from Centene (CNC), and to a much lesser degree Arrow and LKQ (LKQ), every other portfolio company increased EPS in 2025. As we would expect, most of our stocks with good fundamentals had good stock performance. As we show in the top row of the table below, 15 of our current companies, accounting for almost 60% of the portfolio weight, experienced an average of 22% growth in forward earnings, compounded by 18% multiple expansion, leading to an average return of 42%. These winners were still attractively valued at year-end with an average P/E of just 13.6x, a 39% discount to the 22.2x P/E of the S&P 500.

Excludes: CNC, ICLR (ICLR) & LKQSource: Lyrical analysis

Those stocks propelled our returns and are the reason why we had a good 2025. But the year could have been even better if we did not suffer from multiple compression in most of the remaining portfolio. As we show in the bottom row of the table, we had 13 other stocks accounting for 34% of portfolio weight that increased forward earnings by 10% on average in 2025. Yet, despite increasing earnings, those stocks declined due to an average of 17% multiple compression. These companies ended the year at a valuation of just 10.5x, a 53% discount to the S&P 500. If those stocks had just maintained their cheap multiples instead of getting cheaper, that would have contributed over 500 basis points to our returns. Given their fundamentals and now even lower valuation, we believe they could be a major contributor to performance in 2026 and beyond.

Our valuation spread remains wide

We continue to believe our portfolio is well positioned. In the graph below, we show our U.S. portfolio valuation relative to our historical portfolio P/E ratios, represented by the solid and dotted dark blue lines.

Source: FactSet

Our CS composite P/E stands at 12.5x. The S&P 500 P/E, which is represented by the bright blue line, is up to 22.2x, and stands 36% above its average since our inception, 32% above the S&P 500 Equal Weight, and 83% higher than our current EQ portfolio. That is significantly above the spread we observed during the first decade of our firm, when it averaged ~30%.

Our uncommon combination

Not only is our portfolio significantly cheaper than the S&P 500 (shown again below in the bar chart on the left), but our portfolio also has a faster growth profile, as shown in the line graph on the right.

The lines at the top show the EPS growth history of our current CS and EQ composites, while the lines below them show the EPS growth of the S&P 500 and S&P 500 Value.

Dating back to 2007, the S&P 500 has had an annualized EPS growth history of 6.6%. Our current portfolio has a much better history of 10.6% EPS growth, four percentage points faster than the S&P 500.

Our confidence in our ability to outperform is rooted in these two statistics of valuation and growth. If our portfolio’s earnings continue to grow as fast or faster than the S&P 500, we should be able to perform as well or slightly better, as long as our relative multiple stays where it is. But if our companies continue to grow as fast or faster than the S&P 500, the wide discount should justifiably narrow, delivering additional outperformance.

Source: FactSet. See notes below

Conclusion

2025 was a good year for Lyrical in which we produced strong absolute returns of 17.9% matching the S&P 500 despite not owning Magnificent Seven stocks. Moreover, we outperformed both the S&P 500 Value and S&P 500 Equal Weight by 4.7 and 6.5 percentage points, respectively.

Looking forward, the biggest issue we see in the market is the high valuation of the S&P 500. The S&P 500 has outperformed the equal weight index by over 10 percentage points per year for the last three years, which is a record amount. As a result, the S&P 500 looks very expensive, both relative to its own history, and also relative to the S&P 500 equal weight.

If the S&P 500 valuation reverts back to its historical average and in line with the S&P 500 equal weight, we should benefit as we do not own the mega-cap growth stocks that have inflated the index returns and valuation. However, what ultimately should drive performance of Lyrical portfolios is our uncommon combination of both discounted valuation and faster earnings growth. The value spread between our portfolio and the S&P 500 is historically wide at 78%. This compares to about a 30% spread during the first decade of our firm. This spread persists despite the superior growth history of our stocks. Our companies have an average historical EPS CAGR of over 10.6%, four percentage points faster than the S&P 500 growth of 6.6%.

Fundamentals have not always mattered to the market over the last five years. This was evident again in 2025 when 34% of our portfolio had increasing earnings but decreasing multiples. While fundamentals can be ignored in the short term, we maintain our core belief that earnings fundamentals are all that matter in the long term. If our companies keep compounding earnings, their stock prices should follow. Additionally, if their growth continues to match or outpace the S&P 500, their valuations should expand too, increasing their stock prices even more.

That is the Lyrical way.

data.title: VRIG ETF: Income Stability Remains Reasonable
data.description:
Invesco Variable Rate Investment Grade ETF (VRIG) offers an attractive dividend yield thanks to the prolonged macro environment of elevated interest rates. Although the rates are no longer at their maximum level, the combination of risk and return characteristics of the fund remains interesting. My previous coverage on the VRIG ETF underlined the defensive appeal of the fund, which has delivered a 4% return since then. Meanwhile, the fund's investors are still benefiting from high coupon payments and income stability. Factors like economic uncertainty, trade disputes, and geopolitical tension make VRIG features especially reasonable. Considering the uncertain future monetary policy pattern, the VRIG ETF could deliver a decent total return of 4% in 2026.

Fund overview

The Invesco Variable Rate Investment Grade ETF, which I previously covered in May last year, is one of the beneficiaries of the U.S. monetary policy. By tracking investment-grade floaters, the fund provides exposure to the still elevated interest rates along with the credit spread of the most reliable US bonds. The fund booked a 4% total return since my last piece, which set a 4.5% 12-month return target.

My investment thesis on VRIG is built around the defensive properties of the fund, which could be a reasonable instrument to face the market volatility. For reference, the recent swing in the U.S. 10-year treasury yield was caused by the renewed concerns over trade tensions with Europe. The transatlantic disputes merely add up to the ongoing macroeconomic uncertainties and geopolitical tensions, making me reaffirm my Hold rating on the VRIG ETF.

The fund currently earns a 4.83% coupon rate on average from its bond portfolio, which decreased by circa 60 basis points since my previous article. At the same time, the Fed interest rate policy cuts amounted to a total of 75 basis points for the period. However, I believe that VRIG has the potential to benefit further from the interest rate environment.

Following the December 2025 FOMC meeting, the target interest rate was decreased to the 3.75%/3.50% range. We also learned that the Fed sees increased risks from the labour market, believing that the impact of tariffs on inflation will be short-lived. At the same time, there was a slight signal for a likely pause in the future rate cuts.

Looking at the market expectations, the interest rate traders now suggest up to a 50 basis point cut in the target rate over the horizon of the next 12 months. But given the internal split within the committee and the conflicting goals of the Fed's dual mandate, it would not be surprising if the central bank ends up struggling to balance the elevated inflation and the cooling labor market. The dot plot of individual forecasts clearly indicated the hesitation regarding the monetary policy path, which could result in lower than anticipated interest rate cuts. Hence, investors in the VRIG ETF could still benefit from the elevated interest rate environment, which may prove to be for longer than expected.

Although with pauses, the process of interest rate policy normalization in the U.S. would continue. It will find a reflection in the dividend distributions the fund pays to its shareholders from the monthly coupons on securities in the portfolio. The following chart suggests that the dividend payouts began to decline since the beginning of 2025, in line with the interest rates gradually retreating from their peaks.

The current TTM dividend yield stands at 4.93%. Although the curve would continue its downward slope, I expect it to be somewhat less concave, as the fund has a significant exposure to agency bonds. The latter, especially those from GSEs (government-sponsored enterprises), provide additional compensation in the form of default risk and a liquidity premium.

And if we take into consideration the future interest rate pattern as granted, the average rate over the next 12-month period could be around 3.40%. In addition, the average premium of agency bonds over the floating rate notes in the fund's portfolio stands around 0.55%. And although negligible, VRIG's portfolio of securities has an effective duration of 0.15 years, which adds up to a 4% total return I expect the fund to deliver over the next 12-month investment horizon.

Despite the modest upside, with this piece I will maintain my Hold rating on the VRIG ETF. The 4% return would most likely outpace the inflation rate, resulting in a positive real return by the end of the year. And perhaps more importantly, this return comes with low risk, both interest rate and credit. The latter is achieved thanks to the floating rate exposure and the investment-grade securities focus. I believe that these features are especially relevant amid the ongoing macroeconomic and geopolitical uncertainty, coupled with the unclear monetary policy future path.

Looking at the fund's investment flows, the investors invested around $150 million in the fund over the last six months. It could be related to the unfavorable macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop causing volatility in the market. Yet, it doesn't affect the VRIG fund due to the embedded volatility hedge mechanism.

Investment conclusion

The Fed could stretch out the monetary policy easing cycle due to the ongoing economic uncertainty, global trade disputes, and geopolitical tension. These factors could introduce significant volatility to the market, which makes VRIG features especially reasonable. With stable income volatility and elevated interest rates persisting for longer, I believe that the Invesco Variable Rate Investment Grade ETF could be considered a safe haven, with a potential to deliver a 4% return in 2026. The return is in line with that of long-term bond yields but seems to me a more appropriate choice for bond investors amid the current uncertain environment.

data.title: Roper Technologies: At A Crossroad
data.description:
In the fall, I concluded that shares of Roper Technologies (ROP) were approaching interesting territory. The carnage that AI caused among software stocks and a very soft price reaction in response to the fourth quarter results made those shares lose a quarter of their value in just four months.

This came after investors grew even more concerned about the rise of AI and the potential disruption to the business model of Roper, as the underlying results show little impact. While the fourth quarter was nothing special, it was not too bad either, as this and a reasonable outlook for 2026 were used by traders as an opportunity to push shares down much lower.

Currently, a big premium multiple has come down to market multiples, or even less, which looks appealing, although high debt and real AI concerns make it difficult to build conviction to buy the dip in size here.

Focus on Niche

The business model of Roper Technologies is quite well known, focusing on market-leading positions in defensible niches, with business activities focusing on low-capital-intensive businesses, which focus heavily on technology. In this strategy, local management teams are employed in a decentralized organizational structure to foster innovation and improve accountability.

A combination of organic growth and targeted M&A efforts made Roper grow to a $7 billion revenue base in 2024, with these revenues split across three segments. The largest is a $3.8 billion application software business, complemented by a very profitable $1.5 billion network software business and a $1.7 billion technology-enabled business.

Not only has Roper doubled sales of the past decade, all while the company has grown the technology content of the business, it has also expanded already very impressive margins by a few points while incurring very little dilution.

A $170 stock a decade ago peaked near the $600 mark earlier last year, now having settled down to $370 per share, following a near 40% plunge from the highs. This comes as investors fear the impact of AI on software companies and thus key elements of Roper's business model.

Perspective

The enthusiasm in the spring of last year was driven by strong 2024 results, a year in which revenues grew 14% to $7.04 billion, split nicely between organic and inorganic growth, with adjusted earnings reported at $18.31 per share.

The company guided for another 10% increase in 2025 sales, with earnings seen up to $19.75-20.00 per share, as shares at the peak traded around 30 times earnings. Aided by the strong momentum, the company announced a $1.65 billion deal to acquire CentralReach, a deal set to increase net debt to $9.0 billion, equivalent to about 3 times EBITDA.

The company delivered on 5-6% organic growth in the first half of the year, with total reported results seen as a bit stronger thanks to M&A activity. Net debt of $8.6 billion was set to increase a bit as the company announced an $800 million acquisition of Subsplash during the year, this being another bolt-on deal.

Trading at $500 on the final day of September, Roper saw its earnings multiple fall from 30 times at the peak to about 25 times here, providing a 4% earnings yield in combination with a strong track record. Believing that some kind of premium was deserved, I was a bit cautious, given that a 25 times earnings multiple was not cheap, leverage was seen around 3 times, and the overhang of the AI concerns was very real.

Expectations Reset

Given the latest fall to the $370 mark, shares are down to levels last seen in 2023 and levels first seen in 2019! Shares slipped in the middle of October when Roper announced its third-quarter results, with organic sales up 6%. However, shares reacted poorly to the results, with the full-year outlook largely reiterated at the time. Meanwhile, the company upped the dividend by 10%, now payable at $0.91 per share each quarter, for a current roughly 1% dividend yield.

While the company announced the appointment of new executives for freshly created AI roles in November, it was the same AI trend that caused a continued concern for the business and its shares, at least among investors.

Investors were furthermore cautioned with the full-year results, as released towards the end of January, as organic growth for the fourth quarter slowed down to 4%. Full-year reported sales rose by 12% to $7.90 billion, driven by M&A and organic growth, as EBITDA was up 11% to $3.14 billion, with adjusted earnings up 9% to exactly $20 per share.

The company guided for 2026 sales to advance by about 8%, with organic growth seen at 5-6%, and diluted earnings per share expected to be up to $21.30-$21.55 per share. Net debt was reported at $9.0 billion, for a leverage ratio below 3 times, at 2.9 times to be precise.

Net debt did not come down too much after the company bought back $500 million worth of stock, all in the fourth quarter. Some 1.12 million shares were bought back at an average price of $446 per share, as just over a percent of the share count has been bought back over this quarter alone.

What Now?

With shares plunging to $370, the 108 million shares of the company are valued at $40 billion, for a $49 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at around 6 times sales, at 15-16 times EBITDA, with earnings multiples down to 18-19 times based on the 2025 results, and forward earnings multiples down to just 16-17 times here.

Frankly, this indicates that the market is aggressively pricing in these concerns here, with earnings multiples falling from 30 times to 16-17 times in less than a year, although that level remains a bit high. Investors believe that the jury is still out; that is, whether AI will disrupt the business. While this does not seem to be happening anytime soon, based on the 2026 guidance, only looking at the impact this year is highly shortsighted, of course.

I am pleased to see buybacks over M&A, but comments on the conference call of $6 billion in potential M&A and buybacks look aggressive in 2026. I would favor less capital deployment on M&A and instead prefer share buybacks, although that leverage is quite high already for any capital allocation decision.

Frankly, I am in the camp that AI could be a threat to parts of the business, but overall valuations have come in quite a bit, although that leverage is a touch high in my view. Weighing all this together, I am getting more constructive and am contemplating actively allocating to some software names here, potentially including Roper as a prime candidate.

This comes following the great speed of the share price declines in combination with the continued resilient performance. This means that the risk-reward has certainly improved a great deal here.

data.title: Tokyo Electron: A Lot Of Good News Is Priced In
data.description:
Intro

Tokyo Electron (TOELY) (TOELF) is a leading semiconductor equipment firm, with solid market shares in etching and wafer bonding, and a smaller presence in deposition and patterning. The firm owns approximately 12% of the whole WFE Market, and 20% of the DRAM equipment market. Its stock performance has been solid, growing by +85% since my last report in August 2025. Given recent positive CAPEX development in the semiconductor industry, I am raising my estimates but still see limited upside ahead. As a consequence, I would change my rating to a HOLD and would be inclined to take some profit on the stock rally.

Recent business trends

Q3FY26 sales have reached JPY630bn, increasing by +11% YoY and by +14.6% QoQ. By regions, South Korea and Taiwan rebounded sharply. New equipment sales were mainly driven by DRAM and Flash memory applications, which together accounted for close to 40% of sales, up from 30% one year ago.

It is now well known that the data center growth is causing a shortage of memory chips. In an initial phase, memory makers such as Micron (MU) have managed to redirect existing legacy DRAM manufacturing lines into High Bandwidth Memories, but in the end, more CAPEX is needed to fix a structural deficit.

On top of that, the world-leading semiconductor foundry, TSMC, just indicated it will materially increase its near-term CAPEX plan: from $50bn to $56bn in 2026 and from $51bn to $60bn in 2027. This bodes well for the Wafer Equipment market and Tokyo Electron's business.

During the Q2 conference call, management highlighted several interesting points. First, comments on the memory end market were particularly bullish:

Investment in NAND is now picking up after being soft over the past few years. In particular, advanced logic and DRAM for AI applications are driving investment.. For DRAM, investment not only for HBM, but also for commodity DRAM are growing sharply. Double-digit growth is expected to continue next year and beyond.

Flash NAND memory demand in PCs and Smartphones remains steady. However, SSD use in data centers is ramping up quickly. SSDs are replacing HDDs, whose prices have soared amid the severe DRAM shortage.

Recent investment plans for memory indicate a solid outlook for equipment makers. Recently, Micron (MU) just announced a $24 bn capacity investment in Singapore, while SK Hynix said in its FY2025 results that it intends to significantly increase its CAPEX budget in 2026.

The following revenue breakdown shows that Tokyo Electron is well-positioned in the DRAM and NAND memory end markets.

Risks

Semiconductor equipment stocks have been in a bull market over the past year. Of course, a part of this is justified by TSMC and memory maker capital expenditure increases, but we can wonder how long this rally can last.

If we look at the company's forward multiples, we can see that valuations are quite demanding.

Even if we could expect earnings beats along the road, valuation multiples cannot increase indefinitely. Profit-taking in the sector could be wise.

What valuation can we expect?

Using the main hypotheses:

  • A revenue close to $22bn in FY27, or +10% above the “Yen3tn or more” medium-term target of the management, taking into account recent positive news flow on CAPEX spending.
  • FY26 CAPEX should reach $1.6bn due to the completion of two new plants. I then expect a downward normalization for years to come.
  • FCF margins (FCF/revenues) are converging gradually toward 25%, in line with leading semiconductor equipment companies.

With a terminal growth of 3% and a WACC of 9%, I arrive at an updated target price of $130/share, suggesting the stock could be fairly valued.

Conclusion

Tokyo Electron is benefiting from solid CAPEX spending in the semiconductor industry, which has recently accelerated with a major announcement in the foundry and memory segments. Next quarter's results are due on February 6th. Given the positive trends I described earlier, I expect the stock to meet or exceed expectations. However, despite these fundamentals, the current valuation appears stretched relative to peers and recent earnings growth, so I put myself on the HOLD side of the consensus.

data.title: Jack In The Box: Thinking Out Of The Box
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the GreenWood Investors 2025 Letter To Investors.

Every entrepreneur knows the feeling: that moment of despair when the only thing you are aware of is the giant gap between where you find yourself and the life and business you imagine. Once you succeed, people see only the success. If you fail, they see only the failure. Rarely do they see the turning points that could have taken you in a completely different direction. But it's at these inflection points that the most important lessons in business and life are learned. —Stephen Schwarzman

We have more often found highly capable managers being not only receptive, but outright welcoming to our efforts to help on the value creation journey. This was the case this past year with Jack in the Box (JACK), where a CEO has committed to bring the company back to its basic mission.

JACK is a top 20 restaurant brand by sales that had seen its stock fall on hard times since its all-time highs in 2021. We started our engagement with the company towards the end of 2024 as we saw a brand that had strong mindshare, but was being heavily weighed down by the Street's perception of debt concerns, prior capital allocation decisions, its geographic over-index to California, and significant executive turnover. We were intrigued as shares traded for record low valuation multiples, short interest was near all-time highs, and its real estate portfolio was estimated to be worth more than the entire market capitalization.

As we dug deeper, and further engaged with the new CEO, we came to see many of the Street's concerns about JACK as not only fixable, but that the brand could be re-energized through an increased focus on operational excellence, further digital and marketing innovation, and smart capital allocation. Building off of the core customers' love of the menu, we believe there is ample opportunity to grow cash flow and drive multiple expansion.

In the context of being a constructivist, it doesn't necessarily mean that a Greenwood team member needs to be in the board room to help our companies reach the high-end of their potential. Our goal is to provide our portfolio companies and their executives with the supportive skill-sets needed to help super-charge the agenda. In the case of JACK, while we may have been able to help with capital markets, we did not have the operating experience in the quick-service restaurant ("QSR") industry to add much value. We were relying on prior turnaround case studies and experts in the field to help us identify paths to improvement.

In November 2025, Greenwood signed a co-operation agreement with the company to add two new directors to the board. We believe that Alan Smolinisky and Mark King bring far more relevant skills than we could have, and that these new directors have the expertise and ownership mentality to bring a sense of urgency to the boardroom and drive significant future shareholder value.

We have known and worked with Alan for quite some time and he is one of those people you want on your team. We know he will be dogged in his pursuit of balance sheet optimization, capital allocation, and using his owner-operator attitude of “this cannot fail. ” In addition to his deep real estate expertise and value-investing mindset, Alan also disclosed in the 8-K announcing his appointment that he directly owns 169, 349 shares, or roughly 1% of the company’s shares. We are pleased he will be chair of the newly formed Capital Allocation Committee and sleep well knowing we have a savvy owner on the board.

Mark King is an experienced quick-service restaurant CEO, having led Taco Bell for nearly 5 years during the tumultuous Covid-19 era. He oversaw a period of above trend revenue and profit growth with his tenure culminating in being named Restaurant Leader of the Year in 2023 by Restaurant Business. He is an inspirational leader, having led multiple brand turnarounds, and we believe he will bring an aspirational brand vision to the board room.

We know this will not be a linear turnaround given the many challenges facing this company and the QSR industry, but we think JACK has a significantly higher probability of achieving a good outcome relative to 1 year ago, yet the stock price is materially lower. And while fiscal first quarter (January) will still see negative same store sales, we see a roadmap for a re-rating just on the basis that sentiment is so bottomed-out in the QSR sector generally and for JACK in particular. As the company delivers on its fiscal year 2026 guidance of flat same-store-sales, we think JACK might get some new friends on its 75th birthday.

data.title: UnitedHealth: Buying The Dip Could Be A Trap
data.description:
UnitedHealth (UNH) (UNH:CA) is back at it again with a huge fall, finishing almost 20% lower on January 27 after disappointing earnings results and following the almost flat (0.09%) proposed reimbursement rates for Medicare Advantage payers next year.

Is there value in UNH stock? Yes, there's some. Is it worth buying right now? I'm leaning toward no, because buying the dip could trap you into months of choppy price action from here, with no clear upward bias.

I'm sticking with my Hold rating. There are solid reasons to Buy UNH, such as a modest valuation and a potential EPS recovery this year and next, but there are also a good number of reasons to stay away, depending on your investing style.

Personally, unless I'm buying into obvious panic, as I did when I wrote a UNH article on the day of the stock's May 2025 bottom (the panic back then was much worse than today's sell-off), I think the prudent move generally is to look for signs of stabilization before committing to an investment. The story is too messy right now, and the market clearly isn't optimistic about the stock.

My most recent rating on UNH stock was a Hold back in October, as I noted that the risk/reward wasn't as great anymore compared to when the stock was at a low. You can check out that article here.

The market is making it clear that it doesn't like UNH stock right now, and fighting that isn't a great move if you're looking for good returns in the next few months. With the overall market trending higher, there are many other opportunities out there. You don't need to buy a hated stock just because it has value. PayPal (PYPL) investors probably learned that the hard way. In a similar way, UnitedHealth's chart isn't bullish.

Despite oversold conditions, I believe UNH is a Hold at best. Let's get into it.

Disappointing Earnings Results

The earnings results weren't horrible, but there were some parts that investors didn't like. I think one of the most major disappointments was the revenue forecast, which was well below expectations.

The revenue forecast for 2026 is "greater than $439 billion," but as you can see from the screenshot below, the consensus forecast for 2026 was $456.7 billion.

Full-year revenue for 2025 was $447.57 billion, so if 2026 revenue comes in below that, it'd mark the first revenue decline for the company in over three decades.

The adjusted medical care ratio also stayed elevated at 88.9%. It actually increased by 340 basis points from the same period last year.

On the other hand, you have management's adjusted EPS outlook of "greater than $17.75" for this year, which was above the $17.70 consensus for this year. Further, cash flows from operations were 1.5x greater than net income at $19.7 billion. The debt-to-capital ratio also fell by 20bps quarter-over-quarter to 43.9%, and the company is targeting a long-term debt-to-capital ratio of 40% before the end of this year.

Clearly, those numbers weren't enough to excite investors. Management could be under-promising its estimates to set up for a surprise, but I don't want to build a thesis around that right now with all the other uncertainty.

Flat Medicare Advantage Reimbursement Rates

Even before the earnings report, UNH stock was already down quite a bit due to negative news surrounding reimbursement rates. You've probably already read about this in articles, so I'll keep it brief. After-hours on January 26, news came out that the Trump Administration "proposed nearly flat reimbursement rates for Medicare Advantage payers in 2027."

The average payment increase is expected at 0.09%, but per the Seeking Alpha news article linked above:

The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services noted: "When considering estimated risk score trend in MA driven by coding practices and population changes, the expected average change in payments will be 2.54%."

Either way, the issue is that analysts were expecting a 4-6% increase, so this was a big blow that sent health insurance stocks much lower.

In a way, there's a positive twist that can be made here for UNH for the longer-term. Increased pressure on the healthcare industry is likely to hurt smaller players more than it'll hurt larger ones because they don't have the same scale to handle these changes. This can help giants like UNH get more market share over time. Still, these changes directly affect UnitedHealth's forecasts now, and that's what the market actually cares about.

Growth Is Still Expected

Despite the negatives, EPS is still expected to rise this year and next year. If EPS reaches $17.75 this year, that would represent an 8.6% YoY increase. And since management targets "greater than $17.75" in EPS, we can reasonably expect more.

In addition, analysts expected relatively strong growth for 2027, at least as of yesterday before the bad news came out. The estimates haven't been fully updated yet on Seeking Alpha, but as of now, the consensus shows $20.79 in 2027 EPS, which would be a 17.1% increase over the potential $17.75 for 2026.

That estimate may come down, but it seems as though management still expects momentum for 2027. For example, Patrick Conway, the CEO of UNH's Optum Rx, talked about growth for Optum that picks up at the end of this year and extends into 2027 and beyond. Here's what he said during the Q4 earnings call:

Our 2026 performance outlook reflects adjusted earnings growth in all 3 segments of our business, ranging from low to high single-digit year-over-year performance and margin expansion ranging from 20 to 90 basis points across the portfolio. Growth rates will remain somewhat tempered in 2026, but strategic refocus and investment in modern next-generation services should lead to growing momentum in the back half of the year that will carry into 2027 and beyond.

The Valuation Is Still Reasonable, But Is It Enough?

You may look at the forward P/E of 15.9x (based on the share price of $282.7 and a base EPS estimate of $17.75) and think the stock is undervalued. That's a fine assessment, especially since the three-year average forward P/E is closer to 18.5x (see below). But honestly, given all the uncertainty and negative momentum the stock has, I still don't consider a forward P/E of 15.9x to be a steal. It seems reasonable to me. It factors in risks. It doesn't understate them.

But if a relatively low valuation by itself was enough to move a stock, UNH would already be higher right now. Investors are probably going to want to see more clarity, stabilization, and better execution before allowing UNH stock to get out of its sideways trend.

The Chart Isn't Looking Great

If you recall from my last article after they reported Q3 results, I warned about the chart, and the warning ended up playing out. The stock was already showing weakness at that time, with UNH finishing only 0.51% higher after earnings despite being up earlier in the day on positive news. Soon after, the stock broke below an upward trendline I drew, and it never recovered fully after that.

Now, we're still seeing weakness. Not only did the stock finish almost 20% lower, but it finished near the low of the day, showing very little investor enthusiasm. My opinion from years of trading is that if UNH truly was bullish, it wouldn't have finished near today's low. The market likely would have had an easier time "shrugging it off."

Take May 15, 2025, for example. That's when the stock temporarily bottomed on the DOJ probe news. The arrow I drew in the chart below points to May 15. Notice that despite the poor news, the stock finished on a high point, not near lows, leading to a multi-week recovery after. You'll also notice that red days where the stock finished near lows led to more downside, no matter how much everything was "priced in."

There's also a gap to fill in the chart, around $274, which can lead to more downside potential.

I'm not saying that UNH can't bounce in the short term. Anything is possible, especially with the stock being oversold. But I just don't see strong signs to make me say "Buy."

The Bottom Line On UNH Stock

There's no doubt that UnitedHealth is still a dominant company, and it seems to have EPS growth ahead of it now. However, the story is really messy, and the market continues to punish it, as it's expected to see its first revenue decline in over 30 years.

Valuation-wise, it's not an absolute steal, and the chart still isn't bullish. In a market where so many stocks are constantly making new highs, and with those companies having excellent businesses, I just don't see the need to go contrarian mode on UNH stock.

My take: if you value short-term performance, wait for more clarity and for the chart to improve before buying. If you only care about the long term, there's potential here, and it could make sense to buy, but it's still probably not the easiest opportunity out there.

data.title: Commodities: Oil Trades Higher On Iranian Risk
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Oil markets continue to strengthen amid growing concern over a possible escalation between the US and Iran.

Energy: Concerns grow over US/Iranian escalation

Oil prices continue to firm, with ICE Brent settling a little more than 1.2% higher yesterday - its highest level since September. The strength in the flat price and timespreads is at odds with the large oil surplus view. However, a torrent of geopolitical risks, along with recent USD weakness, are supporting the flat price. So are some recent supply disruptions, which are also supporting spreads.

The key uncertainty facing the market is the potential for escalation between the US and Iran. President Trump warned Iran that time is running out to strike a deal, as US ships move towards the region.

Clearly, this more aggressive rhetoric has left the oil market nervous about the potential for supply disruptions. Iran pumps around 3.3m b/d of crude oil, while exporting around 1.5m b/d. This will be the most immediate supply concern.

However, there are also concerns about what this could mean for regional oil supplies. Any escalation may pose a risk to Persian Gulf oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, where around 20m b/d of crude oil passes. In the absence of an escalation, bearish fundamentals should once again take centre stage, leading the market to trend lower.

EIA inventory data shows that US crude oil inventories fell by 2.3m barrels over the last week. This was driven by an 805k b/d decline in imports, while exports grew 901k b/d WoW.

The initial impact from the recent winter storm appears to be reflected in the numbers, with crude oil output in the Lower 48 estimated to have fallen 42k b/d week-on-week, while refineries reduced operating rates by 2.4 percentage points over the week to 90.9%. Refined product stocks saw marginal increases, with gasoline and distillate inventories increasing 223k barrels and 329k barrels, respectively.

In the US natural gas market, the Feb ’26 Henry Hub contract expired yesterday. It had a very volatile final trading session, settling 7.28% higher on the day at $7.46/MMBtu. The expiry of the contract sees the March contract become the front-month contract, which is trading at a little over $3.7/MMBtu (settling 2.3% lower yesterday).

The significant backwardation between the contracts reflects the impact of the recent US winter storm. We continue to see the gas market recovering from this disruption. US natural gas output is trending upwards following the shut-ins seen over the weekend. In addition, gas flows to US LNG plants are recovering, easing concerns about the impact on LNG supplies to global markets.

Metals: Aluminium jumps to record high in Shanghai

Aluminium surged to a record high on the Shanghai Futures Exchange, while LME prices climbed to their strongest level since April 2022. The move comes amid a broader metals rally, supported by a weaker US dollar and tightening supply conditions. Shanghai prices rose nearly 6%, with London gaining close to 2%.

The aluminium market is moving into deficit in 2026, with supply remaining the key constraint. Outside Indonesia, production growth is subdued. China continues to show discipline on capacity additions, and neither Europe nor the US is seeing meaningful smelter restarts. Aluminium prices have also drawn support from the broader rally in copper.

In precious metals, gold continues to surge to record highs, closing almost 4.6% higher yesterday. This strength continued in early-morning trading in Asia, with spot gold hitting a fresh record high of $5,588/oz at one stage.

Geopolitical tensions, a weaker dollar, and investor rotation out of currencies have boosted the precious metal. Gold is now up around 27% year‑to‑date, while silver has gained almost 65%.

ETF flows remain supportive. Total known gold ETF holdings rose by 128koz on 28 January. This marks the sixth consecutive daily inflow and lifting holdings to 100.6moz, the highest level since August 2022.
